prevalence.source: DataSUS and Brazilian epidemiological surveys (e.g., PNS 2019 adjusted)
prevalence.trend: Stable to increasing due to aging population and sedentary lifestyles

incidence.source: IHME GBD Study and Brazilian Ministry of Health reports
incidence.trend: Slight increase linked to occupational factors

burden.source: Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Study 2019, adapted for Brazil

vulnerablePopulations: Low socioeconomic groups in urban slums (favelas)
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis based on history and physical exam (e.g., red flags for serious pathology like cauda equina); imaging (MRI/X-ray) only if indicated; follows international guidelines like NICE or Brazilian Society of Rheumatology

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: Low (~10-20% routine screening in primary care)
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: ~25-30 million with chronic symptoms seeking care
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: ~40-50% effective management in SUS (Sistema Único de Saúde)

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Similar to international standards; emphasis on ruling out infectious causes common in tropical regions (e.g., tuberculosis)
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Multidisciplinary approach via SUS: physiotherapy, analgesics (NSAIDs), education; opioids restricted; referral to orthopedics/rheumatology for refractory cases; follows MoH protocols
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High inequality in access (urban vs. rural); overburdened SUS with long wait times; cultural stigma leading to underreporting; rising obesity epidemic exacerbating cases
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Robust primary care network (Estratégia Saúde da Família) for early intervention; national policies promoting workplace ergonomics; growing research from institutions like Fiocruz
